questions.question: Why is document freshness often discussed separately from ranking freshness in search systems?
options: Because content visibility and ranking-signal quality can evolve on different cadences.
Because ranking never changes after launch.
Because documents do not need versioning.
Because search indexes cannot process deletes.
questions.explanation: The two concerns often have different operational paths.

questions.question: What is a main reason to use tombstones for deletes?
options: They allow fast removal behavior without waiting for a full rebuild.
They make storage free after deletion.
They remove the need for crawl pipelines.
They guarantee identical ranking across all shards.
questions.explanation: Deletes often need a quicker control path.

questions.question: Which metric best captures user-visible update freshness for important content?
options: Document publish lag by priority tier.
Average keyboard repeat speed of clients.
Count of developers editing ranking configs.
Median shard host uptime.
questions.explanation: Publish lag is the direct freshness signal.

questions.question: Why is blue/green or staged rollout helpful for index publishing?
options: It lets you validate and roll back bad index updates before they affect every shard.
It eliminates the need for serving caches.
It makes all ranking features instantly fresh.
It prevents all duplicate documents.
questions.explanation: Safe rollout and rollback are the key benefits.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with snapshot or segment-based publishing instead of mutating serving indexes per document?
options: Serving becomes more stable, but freshness is bounded by publish cadence.
Freshness becomes perfect, but availability drops to zero.
Deletes become impossible.
Ranking signals no longer matter.
questions.explanation: Stable publishes usually trade against instant visibility.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly indicated by high feature-version skew between publishers and serving shards?
options: Ranking inputs and document snapshots are out of sync across the fleet.
Users are typing longer queries.
Crawlers are no longer finding URLs.
Logs are being redacted too early.
questions.explanation: Version skew is a consistency signal for search quality.

questions.question: Why should backfill resources often be isolated from live freshness resources?
options: So bulk reprocessing does not starve urgent new document visibility.
So tombstones never need cleanup.
So ranking features stay static forever.
So serving shards can ignore schema versions.
questions.explanation: Backfills can otherwise crowd out live traffic.

questions.question: What is the better design response when breaking-news freshness becomes more important than perfect ranking quality?
options: Create a faster publish path for high-priority content and accept bounded ranking lag there.
Disable all rollouts and publish checks globally.
Wait for the next full weekly rebuild.
Route every query to the crawler directly.
questions.explanation: Freshness and quality can be traded deliberately by content tier.
